segments.text: Thanks.
All right. We're live. Excellent.
Well, welcome Health New Zealand.
Very good to have you present to us
on the New Zealand Health Plan.
Uh, and to everyone, welcome to the Health Committee.
I'm Sam Dale Committee.
Uh, you'll be familiar with all of the members
around the table here.
Um, I'll let you give a, a quick intro to it,
maybe a few minutes if you, if you will, um, five Max,
and then we'll, we'll move into two members,
ask some questions, and I know members will
have a number of questions.
Sure. Thank you. Well, thank you very much.
We, you are okay too. And thank you very
much for having us along here today.
Uh, we thought we might start with, uh, just a brief summary
of the New Zealand Health Plan
and also talk about some of the limitations that we had
as Health New Zealand at the time
that the plan was produced.
Uh, I won't take you through the whole documents,
but we leave that for you there.
But I thought we might just start by talking on that, uh,
very second slide,
which is Health New Zealand's Planning Landscape.
Uh, so you can see
that the New Zealand Health Plan sits within, uh,
number of other accountability
and planning documents that we do as a health entity.
So those include the statement of performance expectations,
statements of intent, as well as workforce plans,
so health delivery plan.
And then we have medium term, uh, statements as well.
And this document is part of those medium term, um,
uh, documents that we produce for accountability.
I'd make the note too, that the New Zealand Health plan
should be, uh, read alongside the health status report,
the health target implementation plans, uh, as well.
And so they're, they're very strongly connected
into the New Zealand Health Plan.
So as they are the companion pieces,
and they're not repeated within the health plan
because those other documents also exist.
So that planning landscape is complex,
but it's also, uh, quite significant.
And so this piece should be seen within the wider context of
that all our accountability documents
and also the things that we report and publish against.
So that's a little bit about the health plan.
You'll know it's a three year plan.
It's required under prior order,
and it gives effect to the GPS,
the government policy statement.
The other side I just wanted to, um, mention is, uh,
the timing because we,
the New Zealand Health Plan takes effect from
July 1st, 2024,
and of course it hasn't been published, uh, until, uh,
July, 2025.
So I think it's useful just, uh, in terms of context,
the Journey Health New Zealand has been on
because work on this health plan, which is the replacement
for Tata started back in September in 2023.
Uh, of course we had a change of government, uh,
and then we got the GPS, uh, delivered to us in June.
Uh, of course, the health plans to take effect from July.
Uh, so we've got a issue there of timing.
And then in June, July,
we also had significant change within Health New Zealand,
including the appointment of a commissioner
and the need to take into account
the commissioner's priorities.
After that, uh, the plan was finalized in December, uh,
audit New Zealand, uh, then had it,
and then you'll see the minister also needs
to approve the plan,
and then it got published in, um, August.
So that's a little bit of that history
and a bit of an explanation around the timing.
Uh, and then the final thing I'll say is that we, um, accept
that the plan is completely a plan that was done at a period
of time when it was, uh, going
through quite significant change for Health New Zealand.
Our systems are maturing as we go.
One of the issues is the degree to which the plan's able
to be costed.
Uh, and we have said in our, uh, opinion here
that in our view, our systems were not mature enough
to cost it as to potentially the expectation of the office
of the Audited general.
Uh, our view about that is those systems are absolutely
maturing, and we've got a chief financial officer here
that can speak to some of, um,
those issues if there's questions related to that.
So I'll stop there and,
and leave you with the presentation. Thank
You, Dr. Graham. Thank
you for that overview.
I'll come to Aisha first also has a question.
Thank you, um, Mr. Chair. And thanks, Dr. B Brimley.
I'd like to that this, um, the issues
around the health plan
and the desire
to have performance reporting in the health system have a
long history with this committee.
And, um, the auditor general regularly briefs us
as members on the committee on how to do our job in terms
of scrutiny and understanding
how we're getting value for public funds.
And this goes, uh, that input was also
provided by the Auditor General
to the Select committee when the PY R Act was written.
And the change to have an audited plan
or costed plan was, um, part of a response
to the auditor general's feedback at at that point.
So my question is,
what will good look like over time as your systems continue
to mature in terms of the specificity of
linking, um, public investment to,
to outcome?
Um, will we see, uh,
we put this much extra
and then we're able to deliver x, Y, Z additional service?
And to what level of granularity not expecting the world?
What I'm trying to ask is
what should our expectations be in the, in the future?
Hmm. Thank you. So, um, I'll get Bevin to respond shortly,
but I would say between this plan
and where we're at now,
we have seen a significant improvement, both in terms of
how we set our budgets
and the costings related to those budgets.
So we are able to do a top down budgeting process as well
as a bottom up budgeting process.
And that's by district.
Uh, we are also able to, um, move now
to understanding our production planning.
So from a national level that's dropped to regional,
and we hope to go to a district level, uh, as part
of this next round where we really clearly see district
by DI district what they can produce.
One of the things that has changed over time, um, is
that we had quite sophisticated costing systems
that were in place some time ago.
Um, uh, back in the DHB period,
some DHBs costed, well, others didn't.
So we never had a, a good national view of that,
but we definitely were more advanced,
and we are, we've lost some of that.
So part of that is to rebuild costing.
So where am I going to that?
I think you'll see a much closer linkage between
what is the funding available
and what's going to be produced district by district,
class by class.
And we will look to strengthen the costing system.
So we'd much better understand the cost of each
purchase unit being produced.
But, um, I'll hand over, oh, maybe one other thing,
performance reporting.
Um, we want to be able
to release more information more regularly.
So we have the quarterly report, which has 33 metrics in it.
Many of them relate to our accountability documents.
Uh, we are keen to be able to move to release,
um, more regularly.
Again, things like health target reporting on a much more
regular, um, frequency, accepting that
that will be operational data, not
as it were fully quality assured.
And I think as long as people understand the difference,
then we would be keen to do that.
So we are just setting up our systems to be able to look at
that and how we can get it out more regularly.
So we want to get to much more regular
release of information. I'll hand over to Ben.
I think it's very well said, Dale.
Three things that are critical from my perspective,
that you have a budget that is built bottom up,
but meets your top down envelopes to work within,
and that that is owned
by the local districts and and regions.
The second thing is that you are then able
to have integrated performance reporting against that.
And by integrated, you need
to have the operational performance
and the financial hand in hand.
And under, uh, Dale's guidance,
we now have monthly performance reports
with reporting reviews with our regions, which do that,
which bring those two things together.
So you should expect that you see that.
And which flows into the public reporting
that Dale refers to.
The third thing that we would high I would highlight is
that better productivity reporting?
Is that something that you should expect?
So how are we getting better value out
of every dollar that we spend?
We are maturing in that space at the moment.
We now have that integrated into our monthly reviews,
but it is at too much of an aggregated level.
So we need to take that from a regional and district view
and bring it down to theater, local levels.
Yeah. Thanks. They're really compelling answers.
Um, one of the, uh, challenges is
that the health system does so many different things,
and I could imagine you'd be able to come back next year
and the, if you rewrote the plan, you'd have, um,
we're spending this much on hip operations
and we've, um, been able to deliver so many, you,
you could technically do that task.
Um, and there are some high you, uh,
but you could not do that for every,
everything in the health system.
Right? So one of the things I, I assume you can't,
so there's nothing in the document about dialysis services
for existence, for instance,
but I also couldn't imagine a sensible document would get
down to that level of granularity.
So could you please describe how you'll ma manage
that challenge in the future?
Will you be picking some representative outputs
and how would you, how would you decide what they are?
Yeah, so we are moving that way internally now.
So, so for example,
there's obviously a very significant focus on the health
targets, but we also try to understand balancing measures
around each of those targets.
And then we are now looking at a suite of diagnostic, um,
waiting times and understanding
how diagnostic waiting times are moving
and getting much more regular reporting on that.
In terms of things like elective surgery
or FSAs, uh, we would look at major class
for reporting against, so both volume
and if we can improve our costing systems to a point,
at least have a selection of those, which we, we can get
to a costed view of some
of those high level procedures like hips, knees, cataracts.
So we would pick up tension.
The thing is to look at the very high volume ones. Mm-hmm.
Or there's, uh, the, the ones which are potentially, um,
high risk, uh, if they're left, um,
such as cardiac surgery, where we want
to keep a very close eye on those groups as well.
And just, um, uh, last up on this Mr.
Chair, uh, the, um,
document mentions how these enable, um, decisions
around trade-offs, which is the reality of how we have
to allocate resources in the health system.
How are you able to think about that over, say,
a 10 year horizon?
Some of the big changes
that are necessary will take a long, a long time.
So, um, what do you need to be able to, uh,
to be able to do that to, how, how might you plan,
for example, to bring, um, more resource into primary care
and out of the hospital, over out
of hospitals over time and achieve that?
So, that's a very good question.
I, I, I'd answer it in several ways.
I, I've been doing planning
and funding things in the health sector for 25 years,
and within one to two years, usually our, um,
planning environment changes quite significantly.
It's, it's a, so having a, a stable
long-term view of what we are trying to achieve
and the funding that would go with that,
I think is very important.
Um, because if we don't have those two things,
we are in a much more dynamic situation where as soon
as you can produce something, it can be out
of date within the year.
And that's the potential issue with a long-term plan is
that dynamic environment changes.
And so a lot of your assumptions that you made in
that plan will change.
So I think stability around that is the first thing.
Uh, being, having signals about longer term fundings
important for us so that we can future forward focus our,
our production plans,
and so we can understand what we can produce over time.
But the, um, third thing I would say
that in the health system, it is much more difficult to
disinvest, um, much easier to invest and add to things.
And when we are in a, uh, potential for disinvestment
or for a change in services, then, um, you need,
uh, a basically a significant coalition of the willing
around, um, that if we need to disinvest to reinvest.
And so those environments are very difficult, I would say.
Um, and it's understandable too,
because if you have public services,
people are very attached to those services
and those delivery, and they don't want change.
And, and we get that. So part of that's for us
to produce a compelling reason of why something
new might be a lot better.
So we need to be able to do that and articulate that.
The final thing I'd say, um,
at the moment we are probably on the cusp
of some quite significant
and, uh, changes in healthcare moving forward.
We are seeing the advent of AI enhanced technologies.
We have just started, uh, the release of something called,
um, uh, some AI ambient scribes in our hospitals.
I've never seen something taken up so fast
inside the health sector mm-hmm.
Uh, where the clinical demand is so extraordinary high.
So we're up to 700, um, clinicians using it,
another 500 waiting.
Um, those technologies, uh, you know, we have to go
through a very careful assessment of them,
but they will significantly change, I think some
of our productivity and some of the advances in cancer,
cancer medicines, uh, the way that we, um,
think about where does care need to be delivered, um,
we'll come to our view about can we deliver much more care
and straight into people's home with supports straight
to the home, which now
that's a really significant model shift, right?
'cause 'cause we're so used to our view about
what hospitals do, what primary care does.
So we have to build into these plan basically
a very clearer articulation of what's coming
and how over time we can shift.
And it's a multi-year shift,
and I think we need to carve out places in the
plan for that going forward. Thank
You. I've got
Ricardo and then Hamish. Thank you.
I've just got a question in a couple of subs
to follow it up, but noting your remarks
around the interconnectedness
of the New Zealand health plan, as well
as some other shortterm shorter term documents
like the health workforce plan.
Can I ask whether, um, from your perspective, um,
whether you think that safe staffing levels play a role in
achieving the goals
that you laid out in the, you see on health plan?
And if so, how are you hoping
to achieve safe staffing levels in order to meet the goals
that you've, um, put in place in the health plan as well?
So the workforce is a key enabler of the entire plan.
Um, we can't deliver any of the plan without our workforce,
and they, they are our single most valuable
resource in Health New Zealand.
So I think that would be my opening view.
Um, we have some of the best
and most highly trained workforces in the world.
Um, we are very privileged to have them working with us.
The second point I would make about the, uh, the plans is
that we then produce health workforce plans,
which should be seen as companions to this,
both mental health and gen, general health workforce plans,
which show us where our priorities are, uh,
around those workforces
and where we are trying to get to, uh, in terms
of both development of our workforce
and also where the investment priorities are.
And then I would say in terms of safe staffing, uh, a lot
of work is going on
and we've got Richard, our chief,
probably go into a bit more detail for you.
Um, trying to understand the right mix of staffing
for the acuity of the patients that we see in hospitals.
Safe staffing has got a particular connotation
to it at the moment, but when I look at it from the,
the lens, I see that quality care is made up
of multiple dimensions, access, effectiveness, efficiency,
equity, as well as safe.
And so it's not just one person, it's how the team works
to care for a patient and their family.
Uh, undoubtedly that really relates
to our health workforce plan.
Yeah. And add to that, um,
I think this question of safe care really.
So, um, we work very closely with HQ C around a series
of metrics that assess the safety of care in our hospital,
pressure injuries, um, you know, uh,
infection rates, et cetera, et cetera.
And so we look at those metrics regularly to really assess
how safe our hospitals, we are part of, uh,
health round table, uh, which is a data set,
which gives us a view now across the whole country.
So it gives us a real ability to go, okay, where are areas
where, you know, issues or risks are arising,
and how do we kind of get involved in that space to look at
what the staffing model's like,
but also the real opportunity, uh, to kind of shift
to a more interprofessional, um, model of care.
So looking at different workforce models,
particularly Allied Health, the around the one just front
and center right now would be
around the muscular fetal pathway.
So patients who have, you know, um, back injuries
or have poor shoulder injuries, getting them
to see advanced physios, uh, bringing 'em into the system,
uh, and then making sure we've got our surgeons working on
the patients where the care is the greatest benefit.
So it is to, to, um, today point quite a complex space,
but there's some real, um, important metrics that we look at
around safety care, staffing.
Um, and I just reiterate Dale's point.
Um, our workforce is our number one, you know, pardon.
And this opportunity as we've moved away from 20 districts
to regions to kind of collectively try
and solve these problems
so we can deliver better care for everybody.
Will you be using the care
and capacity demand management data as part of, um,
measuring your performance in that area?
So we do use that particularly in our wards.
So, um, that's a, a tool that's been,
as you be aware around for a long time.
And so we use that along our wards.
We are, um, working with our colleagues, unions, et cetera,
around reviewing.
Um, it's because it,
it's works well in some spaces, but not in all spaces.
So our EDS is a challenging space to, to make that,
that tool actually work out
what we need from a safety perspective.
Um, but yeah, certainly it's still part of our, our package
of, of measures to deliver our care
And looking at that data set more importantly,
what do you think it tells you in terms of, um, the,
the work ahead when it comes to safe staffing levels?
I mean, looking at some of those data sets,
it looks pretty dire.
Do you acknowledge that, um,
the dataset paints a grim picture
and that, you know, better, better, um, transparency
and release of that information can give the public
and the workforce confidence that, um,
those issues are being addressed?
Uh, I guess I'll bring it back to my earlier comments
that, um, sort of we look at safe care.
So, and staffing is a component of safe care,
but there are many components of safe care.
So I think that yes, um,
those metrics are helpful in helping to try
and create that entire picture,
but it's not the only, uh, component of it.
So I would, I would, um, I guess challenge the comment that,
uh, that's the answer to, to, to safe care.
I think it's a component of safe
Care, but it is an Important mate, uh, hold on.
We'll come through the chair, please.
Through the chair. It is an important data point, though,
in order to measure performance.
It, I accept that it's not the only one,
but that you acknowledge it's not the less,
quite an important data point's.
One of the data points that we have to assess safety,
Please. Thank you, Dr. Kim.
Excellent. Brilliant. Thank you.
Um, and I appreciate Health New Zealand is probably one
of the more complex, uh,
organizations we have here in, in New Zealand.
Um, and I, so I appreciate the work that's gone in.
We have heard previously, um, in this committee that
the combined district health wards have a lower output than
individual, uh, district health wards.
How is this plan going to, um,
help our efficiency in the healthcare system?
Put another set? Yeah, go for it.
Oh, well, I'll start. And then Bevin.
So the health targets, for example,
in the plan are very tear clear articulation over time of
where we are aiming in terms of milestones
and then what the target is.
And then we also have volume information that we expect
to deliver, uh, by our services and here.
So I think there are those plans,
and then you need to read them in, uh, association
with the health target implementation plans,
which we have both for the health targets
and for the mental health targets, right?
So, uh, if you read them as a combined,
you'll see the actions that need to be undertaken, uh,
under each health target
and what we think that will achieve.
So with that in mind, I think, um, if we start
to look at how we are doing in terms of this last year,
and we've just released our Q4 report, uh,
so on those health targets, uh, we are now starting
to see some movement.
So, um, for example, minimization has gone from 76 to 82%.
We're still a long way short,
but we've seen that trajectory turn the first time in
several years since 20 20, 21.
We've seen the FSA and elective target got achieved in Q4.
Uh, so that was, uh, very pleasing.
We've seen cancer target also achieved
and we're 0.1% off the ED target.
We are also looking how we've performed for each
of those over a series of years.
And you can see now an upward trajectory, um, that some
of those improvements started around January, February,
because we have some very long waiting lists
and we've been trying to bring them down.
So what you will see is, um, movements in those indicators
and those targets in particular,
because we are very focused on them, as I said,
we wanna be mindful of balancing measures
and think about them and be able to release,
uh, that information.
So it's not the, uh, only thing we,
and we are trying to hold them into context within
terms of health outcomes.
And you'll also see, uh,
and when we release our quarterly reports is those 33
measures, you'll be able to see how they're tracking
and what's, uh, um,
and the narrative, what is, uh, uh, being associated to,
to get to those improvements.
Okay. Give us, Uh, I would just add quickly, um,
productivity across the health system has
been in decline for 10 years.
In the past 12 months is the first time we've seen
that stabilize and start to, uh, improve.
Um, so that's done under the collective system.
I think if I could use the example of infrastructure
as well, we're now starting to see examples
where we have like projects across multiple parts of, uh,
the coun country Minister Brown announced, uh,
just last week, the temporary inpatient, uh, units
that we're putting into five of the districts.
We'll use a single, uh, constructor to do that.
We'll use standardized design.
So there are opportunities like that as well,
which didn't exist previously under, under the districts.
Mm-hmm.
Um, and just kind of really following up on the
infrastructure piece, uh,
what about the IT infrastructure
is, uh, we're going forward?
Um, appreciate the, it's an 85 page document to cover one
of the most complex, uh, organizations in the country.
So not everything can be,
can be spelled out whereabouts is kind of the plan
around the it it systems to have the integrated,
um, approach across the country.
We, um, we've as you know, published the, um,
on the physical infrastructure,
our 10 year health infrastructure plan earlier this, um,
this year, which is an important
part of our long-term planning.
And we're very close to publishing our 10 year digital, uh,
investment, uh, priorities over that.
Uh, over that period, um, Dale has, has referenced the power
of those digital investments
to really enable frontline improvements in
care and productivity.
As, as we've worked on that plan, we see a lot
of those opportunities
and they need importantly, to be done as part
of a consistent framework.
So we're pleased that we'll be able to publish
that plan shortly, which will show the roadmap
of investments we think are important, uh, to Dale's point
as we embrace what is really a cusp of change in
how care is is delivered.
So that will be out shortly.
Dr. Ll Um,
you were very polite earlier when I asked about planning,
and you pointed out the planning environment is difficult
because of changes in the environment.
You didn't say that it was politicians that caused them, um,
which is not kind of you,
but, um, that's probably the truth.
And I wondered if, um, you had thought about
how getting agreed priorities
across political cycles could be enabled by transparency.
So, um, has just done a, doing a report into
dementia beds, for example,
and we've learned that there's no, um,
long-term projections owned by the health system, uh, of,
um, the number of dementia beds we need.
Clearly, we can't do our job of thinking about
how we get tore, create the policies that support getting to
that place without, um, information about
what these long-term needs and challenges are.
So you see planning
or other types of reporting as being able to support clear
articulation of what New Zealand's
needs are in the long term.
So in terms of, um, healthcare planning, the, you know,
the traditional, um, way that we would think about
that is in terms of, um, health needs of your population,
how that's changing over time, our capacity
to respond in terms of our providers, public, private
community, NGOs, uh,
and then an overlay of, you know, where is healthcare going?
And try and bring that all together
and say, uh, for a planning,
here's our priority areas based on epidemiology,
based on health needs, based on patient
and provider feedback.
And then, um, uh, would,
what would a investment program look like to
improve health outcomes related to those classes?
Right. Um, I think if there was, um, consistency
of alignment about population needs
and responses to them, I think
that would be a very good thing.
The other thing I would say is often there's difference in,
um, style but not substance.
Because actually, for example,
when we do the healthcare needs, we know
that the most significant, uh, cause of death
for New Zealanders is still
cardiovascular disease and cancer.
Uh, we know that the risk factors
are still the risk factors.
Uh, the thing that's probably a lot more dynamic now is
these technologies, uh,
and the relative emphasis that goes on to different things.
I think if you had a longer term plan
where there was alignment around that,
it would be, uh, very good.
Well, that's, that's, um, reassuring.
So one of the things that, um, we go to some of the areas
of more specific planning, the, um, infrastructure plan.
Uh, it does not contain bed numbers.
Now I'm all on board if we're gonna have modern ways
of housing patients that are not beds,
but I'm pretty sure beds are still gonna be important
for the next decade.
How can we really be saying we're meaningfully doing
planning if we're putting out an infrastructure plan
that lists the number of car parks,
but not the number of hospital beds?
Alright, I'd answer that in several ways.
So, um, again, have a, have a long history
of doing the modeling work for hospital beds.
And, um, invariably once we produce the modeling work
doesn't come to full fruition, the bed requirements.
And the reason for that is the earlier issue about the
dynamic nature of changes within the healthcare system, uh,
meaning some of the assumptions that went into
that bed modeling has changed. Are
You saying that you retrofit fit the number of beds
to the budget because that that is a political decision?
No. And so the information is really important
for politicians in government and opposition
because there's are long term yes assets for the public.
It's important we're able to think about that
before the budget has drawn a line across
how many beds are allowed.
Yes. No, I wasn't heading there.
What, what, what is meaning is that we can do the modeling
of the projections based on the current
demand, that's for sure.
And then what we can do is basically counterfactual lines
saying, what's coming down the pathway that might change
that bed number needed?
Sure. And so in those, what
what I'm getting at right now is in those counterfactual
lines right now, it's become much more difficult to project
partly because of what is occurring in a number
of places is this very significant movement to be able
to care for people in other environments.
So, uh, home care, uh, a number
of our hospitals now already are
delivering services in the home.
Uh, the idea that other places might be able to be sort
of intermediary, uh, areas.
So you might not need the full intensity of a full
hospital tertiary site.
And then to your earlier point,
you will always need hospitals and hospital beds.
So I think it's just the, the dynamic of where
that care gets there delivered is starting to change.
And our view about how much we can
deliver into people's homes.
So not arguing with the idea of, um,
care in people's homes.
And I understand the point that all forecasts
or projections have uncertainty around them,
but can't we have a proper public conversation about
that and have those estimates published?
Because how, how on earth would we, would we know how
to build assets with a, that are meant
to last a generation if we're not as politicians able
to be talking about what that 10, 20 year horizon
of investment is?
So surely, surely an infrastructure plan has
to have bed numbers in it.
Uh, I see. I think what we would, um,
in our health service planning piece, which
can have those projections in there, I think
that's the linkage to the health infrastructure plan.
So what's in the health infrastructure plan is much more the
nuts and bolts of the infrastructure.
I think what you, you, what I would've thought of is
what you've just described is our house service planning
that informs that infrastructure plan, which should yes.
Have be modeling in it.

6 November 2025 | Health Committee

Briefing on the New Zealand Health Plan
Health New Zealand—Te Whatu Ora
